title: How Machines Learn a Pattern
concept: Training a model isn't one lucky guess — it's a loop: the model guesses, checks how wrong it was, adjusts itself a little, and repeats that loop many times until its guesses get close.

exercises.prompt: You're seasoning a pot of soup. You add a pinch of salt, taste it, decide it needs more, add a bit more, and taste again. What's the name for this taste-adjust-taste cycle?

options.text: Trial and error
Following a fixed recipe exactly
Guessing once and never checking again

exercises.explain: Right! Tasting, adjusting, and tasting again is trial and error — you use feedback each round to get closer to the right answer.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If your first guess is wrong, adjusting a little and trying again usually gets you closer to the right answer than giving up after one try.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! Small adjustments based on feedback, repeated, tend to close the gap between a wrong guess and the right answer.

cards.title: Mia bakes a batch of cookies
cards.body: Mia guesses "3/4 cup of sugar" for her cookie dough. She bakes a test batch, tastes it — too bland. She adds a bit more sugar, bakes another test batch, tastes again — closer! She repeats this a few more times until the cookies taste just right.

cards.title: What is training, exactly?
cards.body: Training a model means repeating a loop: the model makes a guess, checks how far off (wrong) that guess was, adjusts itself slightly to do better, and repeats — often thousands of times.

cards.title: Tasting the soup
cards.body: Training a model is like tasting soup as you cook: guess how much salt to add, taste it, decide if it needs more or less, adjust, and taste again. Each round gets you a little closer to "just right."

cards.title: Guessing a house price
cards.body: A model guesses a house is worth $200,000. The real price is $250,000 — a big error. It adjusts and guesses $230,000 next time — closer! It keeps adjusting, round after round, until its guesses land near the real prices.

cards.title: It doesn't get it right on the first try
cards.body: It's tempting to picture a model instantly "knowing" the answer. In reality, its very first guesses are usually way off. Getting good takes many, many rounds of guess-check-adjust — not a single flash of insight.

cards.title: One guess vs. a loop of guesses
cards.body: Two very different ways to arrive at an answer:

exercises.prompt: A model guesses a house is worth $180,000, but it actually sold for $220,000. What should the model do next in the training loop?

options.text: Adjust itself to reduce that error, then guess again
Stop training since one guess is enough
Ignore the error and move to a completely different house
Delete the house from its memory

exercises.explain: Exactly! Checking the error and adjusting is the whole point of the loop — it's how the model's guesses improve over time.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A model only needs to run through the guess-check-adjust loop one single time to make accurate predictions.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! One pass is rarely enough. Models typically repeat the loop many times, gradually shrinking their errors.

exercises.sentence: The training loop is: guess, check the ___, adjust, and repeat.
exercises.explain: The model checks its error (how wrong the guess was) so it knows which direction to adjust in.

exercises.prompt: Which one shows a model going through its training loop?

options.text: A model guesses $150k for a house, sees it's $30k off, and nudges its next guess higher
A model outputs the exact same $150k guess for every house, forever
A programmer writes a fixed rule that all houses cost $150k

exercises.explain: Correct! Checking the error and nudging the next guess is exactly the guess-check-adjust loop in action.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Making a small adjustment after each error, repeated over many rounds, generally moves a model's guesses closer to the correct answers.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! Small consistent adjustments, repeated many times, are how the errors shrink round by round.

exercises.prompt: A model is being trained to guess a person's age from a photo. Round 1: it guesses 40 for someone who is actually 25 (way too high). Round 2: after adjusting, it guesses 30 (closer). What is happening between round 1 and round 2?

options.text: The model used its error from round 1 to adjust and produce a better guess in round 2
The model memorized the exact answer and will always be right now
The model ignored round 1 completely and guessed randomly
The photo itself changed between rounds

exercises.explain: Exactly! Round 2's improvement comes directly from adjusting based on round 1's error — that's the training loop working.

exercises.prompt: Two teams are building models. Team A's model stops after a single guess with no adjustment. Team B's model repeats guess-check-adjust for thousands of rounds. Which team's model is more likely to end up making accurate predictions, and why?

options.text: Team B, because repeating the loop lets errors shrink gradually over many rounds
Team A, because one guess is always enough for a computer
They'll perform identically since the number of rounds doesn't matter
Team A, because more rounds always confuses a model

exercises.explain: Right! Repeating the loop is what lets a model's guesses improve — a single untested guess rarely lands anywhere close to correct.
